metadata.title: Clinical Trial Endpoints for the Approval of Cancer Drugs and Biologics

rules.rule_text: New drugs must demonstrate effectiveness based on substantial evidence derived from adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations to support NDA, BLA, or supplemental application approval.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Statutory and Regulatory Requirements for Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: This law requires substantial evidence of effectiveness and specifies that this evidence must be derived from adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations.
rules.exceptions: In certain cases, evidence from a single adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation with confirmatory evidence can be sufficient.
cross_references: Federal Food Drug and Cosmetic Act 1962 amendment

rules.rule_text: Accelerated approval may be granted based on an effect on a surrogate endpoint or intermediate clinical endpoint that is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit, for drugs intended to treat serious or life-threatening diseases that demonstrate improvement over available therapy or provide therapy where none exists.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Statutory and Regulatory Requirements for Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: The accelerated approval regulations (21 CFR part 314, subpart H and 21 CFR part 601, subpart E), promulgated in 1992, allow use of additional endpoints for approval of drugs or biological products that are intended to treat serious or life-threatening diseases and that generally demonstrate an improvement over available therapy or provide therapy where none exists.

rules.rule_text: A drug approved under accelerated approval regulations must have the manufacturer conduct postmarketing clinical studies to verify and describe actual clinical benefit; FDA may withdraw approval if postmarketing studies fail to demonstrate clinical benefit or if the applicant does not demonstrate due diligence.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Statutory and Regulatory Requirements for Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: A drug is approved under the accelerated approval regulations on condition that the manufacturer conducts clinical studies to verify and describe the actual clinical benefit. If the postmarketing studies fail to demonstrate clinical benefit or if the applicant does not demonstrate due diligence in conducting the required studies, among other reasons, FDA may withdraw approval of the drug or indication.

rules.rule_text: In many cases, at least two adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations are needed to establish effectiveness for cancer drug approval.
rules.source_section: Section II.A — Statutory and Regulatory Requirements for Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: In many cases, at least two adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations are needed. In certain cases, evidence from a single adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation, with confirmatory evidence, can be sufficient (e.g., in cases in which a single multicenter study provides highly reliable and statistically strong evidence of an important clinical benefit).
rules.exceptions: A single adequate and well-controlled investigation with confirmatory evidence may suffice if it provides highly reliable and statistically strong evidence of an important clinical benefit.
cross_references: FDA guidance Providing Clinical Evidence of Effectiveness for Human Drug and Biological Products

rules.rule_text: Overall survival (OS) is defined as the time from randomization until death from any cause and must be measured in the intent-to-treat population; it is considered the most reliable cancer endpoint and the preferred endpoint when studies can adequately assess survival.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Overall Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Overall survival is defined as the time from randomization until death from any cause and is measured in the intent-to-treat population. Survival is considered the most reliable cancer endpoint, and when studies can be conducted to adequately assess survival, it is usually the preferred endpoint.
rules.exceptions: Difficulties include long follow-up periods and subsequent cancer therapy potentially confounding survival analysis.

rules.rule_text: Overall survival should be evaluated in randomized controlled studies; data derived from externally controlled trials are seldom reliable for time-to-event endpoints including overall survival.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Overall Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Overall survival should be evaluated in randomized controlled studies. Data derived from externally controlled trials are seldom reliable for time-to-event endpoints, including overall survival.

rules.rule_text: When the primary study endpoint is based on tumor measurements (e.g., PFS or ORR), tumor assessments generally should be verified by central reviewers blinded to study treatments to ascertain lack of assessment bias.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Endpoints Based on Tumor Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: When the primary study endpoint is based on tumor measurements (e.g., PFS or ORR), tumor assessments generally should be verified by central reviewers blinded to study treatments (see Appendix) to ascertain lack of assessment bias.
rules.exceptions: Centralized independent verification may not be necessary when randomized trials are blinded (unless adverse event profile would substantially unblind the trial) or effect sizes are robust in large randomized trials where sensitivity analysis supports lack of investigator bias.

rules.rule_text: Tumor assessment endpoint selection requires two judgments: determination of whether the endpoint may support accelerated or traditional approval, and evaluation of the potential for bias or uncertainty.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Endpoints Based on Tumor Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Tumor assessment endpoint selection should include two judgments. First, a determination of whether the endpoint may support either accelerated approval or traditional approval should be ascertained. Second, the endpoint should be evaluated for the potential of bias or uncertainty.

rules.rule_text: Disease-free survival (DFS) is defined as the time from randomization until disease recurrence or death from any cause; the protocol must carefully delineate both the definition of DFS and the schedule for follow-up assessments and visits.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1 — Disease-Free Survival (and Event-Free Survival)
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, DFS is defined as the time from randomization until disease recurrence or death from any cause. The most frequent use of this endpoint is in the adjuvant setting after definitive surgery or radiotherapy.

rules.rule_text: Objective response rate (ORR) is defined as the proportion of patients with tumor size reduction of a predefined amount and for a minimum time period; stable disease should not be a component of ORR.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.2 — Objective Response Rate
rules.verbatim_quote: ORR is defined as the proportion of patients with tumor size reduction of a predefined amount and for a minimum time period. Response duration usually is measured from the time of initial response until documented tumor progression. Generally, the FDA has defined ORR as the sum of partial responses plus CRs. Stable disease should not be a component of ORR.

rules.rule_text: The ORR response criteria should be predefined in the protocol before the start of the study, with standardized criteria used to ascertain response.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.2 — Objective Response Rate
rules.verbatim_quote: If available, standardized criteria should be used to ascertain response. A variety of response criteria have been considered appropriate (e.g., revised RECIST guideline (version 1.1)). The response criteria should be predefined in the protocol before the start of the study.

rules.rule_text: Complete response (CR) is defined as no detectable evidence of tumor, generally measured through imaging studies or histopathologic assessment.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3 — Complete Response
rules.verbatim_quote: CR is defined as no detectable evidence of tumor. CR is generally measured through imaging studies (e.g., CT scans) or through histopathologic assessment (e.g., bone marrow biopsy or breast cancer resection specimens).

rules.rule_text: Progression-free survival (PFS) is the preferred regulatory endpoint over time to progression (TTP); PFS is defined as the time from randomization until objective tumor progression or death, whichever occurs first, and the precise definition of tumor progression must be carefully detailed in the protocol.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 — Time to Progression and Progression-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Compared with TTP, PFS is the preferred regulatory endpoint. PFS includes deaths and thus can be a better correlate to overall survival... The precise definition of tumor progression is important and should be carefully detailed in the protocol.

rules.rule_text: PFS data collection for each assessment visit should be limited to a specified short time interval around the scheduled visit to avoid difficulties in determining the event date and censoring date when data are collected over a prolonged time period.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 — Time to Progression and Progression-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Because progression data can be collected from multiple sources (including physical exams at unscheduled visits and radiological scans of various types) and at different times, data collection for each assessment visit should be limited to a specified short time interval around the scheduled visit.

rules.rule_text: Plans for PFS data collection and analysis should be discussed with FDA at end-of-phase 2 meetings.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 — Time to Progression and Progression-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: Plans for PFS data collection and analysis should be discussed with FDA at end-of-phase 2 meetings.

rules.rule_text: Time to treatment failure (TTF) is generally not recommended as a regulatory endpoint for new molecular entity drug approval.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.5 — Time to Treatment Failure
rules.verbatim_quote: TTF is generally not recommended as a regulatory endpoint for new molecular entity drug approval.

rules.rule_text: For improvement of signs and symptoms or other clinical outcome assessments (COA) to be used as efficacy endpoints for cancer drug approval, symptoms should be assessed that are due to cancer rather than drug toxicity to the extent possible.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — Endpoints Involving Symptom Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: For the improvement of signs and symptoms or other clinical outcome assessments (COA) to be used as efficacy endpoints to support cancer drug approval, symptoms should be assessed that are due to cancer rather than drug toxicity to the extent possible.
cross_references: FDA guidance Patient Reported Outcome Measures: Use in Medical Product Development to Support Labeling Claims

rules.rule_text: The use of a symptom palliation endpoint requires that the population be symptomatic at baseline.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.1 — Specific Symptom Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of a symptom palliation endpoint requires that the population be symptomatic at baseline, which can be problematic in many cancer trials where patients can often be asymptomatic at baseline.

rules.rule_text: A composite symptom endpoint or symptom scale should have components of similar clinical importance and an analysis of the contribution of the components should be submitted with the primary analysis of the overall composite endpoint.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.1 — Specific Symptom Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: A composite symptom endpoint or symptom scale should have components of similar clinical importance and an analysis of the contribution of the components should be submitted with the primary analysis of the overall composite endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Procedures should be put in place to maximize completion rate during trial conduct and the statistical analysis plan should outline how missing symptom data will be handled.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.2 — Problems Encountered with Symptom Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Procedures should be put in place to maximize completion rate during trial conduct and the statistical analysis plan should outline how missing data will be handled.

rules.rule_text: Data collection to support multiple symptom endpoints should be addressed prospectively regarding multiple hypotheses testing and the necessary statistical adjustments should be specified in the statistical analysis plan (SAP).
rules.source_section: Section III.C.2 — Problems Encountered with Symptom Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Data collection to support multiple symptom endpoints should be addressed prospectively regarding multiple hypotheses testing and the necessary statistical adjustments should be specified in the SAP.

rules.rule_text: If a sponsor is planning to use an emerging endpoint in its clinical development program, FDA recommends discussing such use with the applicable FDA Division or Office prior to initiating a trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.E — Emerging Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: If a sponsor is planning to use an emerging endpoint in its clinical development program, we recommend discussing such use with the applicable FDA Division or Office prior to initiating a trial (see section V).

rules.rule_text: Per 21 CFR 314.126, studies must allow a valid comparison to a control and must provide a quantitative assessment of the drug's effect; the most reliable method for demonstrating efficacy is to show a statistically significant improvement in a clinically meaningful endpoint in randomized controlled trials.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Clinical Trial Design and Analysis Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Per 21 CFR 314.126, the FDA approves drugs based on substantial evidence of efficacy from 'adequate and well-controlled investigations,' as described in the regulations. Studies must allow a valid comparison to a control and must provide a quantitative assessment of the drug's effect.

rules.rule_text: In settings where there is no available therapy and major tumor regressions can be presumed attributable to the tested drug, FDA has sometimes accepted ORR and response duration observed in single-arm studies as substantial evidence supporting accelerated approval.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — Single-Arm Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: In settings where there is no available therapy and where major tumor regressions can be presumed to be attributed to the tested drug, the FDA has sometimes accepted ORR and response duration observed in single-arm studies as substantial evidence supporting accelerated approval.
rules.exceptions: Single-arm trials do not adequately characterize time-to-event endpoints such as OS, DFS, TTP, or PFS; a randomized study is necessary to evaluate time-to-event endpoints.

rules.rule_text: A noninferiority (NI) trial must demonstrate the new drug's effectiveness by showing it is not less effective than the active control by a prespecified noninferiority margin that is a clinically acceptable loss not larger than the effect of the active control drug.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B — Randomized Studies Designed to Demonstrate Noninferiority
rules.verbatim_quote: A noninferiority (NI) trial should demonstrate the new drug's effectiveness by showing that the new drug is not less effective than a standard regimen (the active control) by a prespecified amount (noninferiority margin). This noninferiority margin should be a clinically acceptable loss that is not larger than the effect of the active control drug.
rules.exceptions: NI trials with endpoints other than OS and ORR are problematic.
cross_references: FDA guidance Non-Inferiority Clinical Trials to Establish Effectiveness
ICH E10 Choice of Control Group and Related Issues

rules.rule_text: For radiotherapy and chemotherapy protectant trials, the design must include two objectives: assessment of amelioration of cancer treatment toxicity, and determination of whether anticancer activity is compromised by the protectant.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C — Trial Designs for Radiotherapy Protectants and Chemotherapy Protectants
rules.verbatim_quote: Radiotherapy protectants and chemotherapy protectants are drugs designed to ameliorate the toxicities of therapies. These trials usually have two objectives. The first is to assess the amelioration of cancer treatment toxicity. The second objective is to determine whether anticancer activity is compromised by the protectant.

rules.rule_text: The methodology for assessing, measuring, and analyzing the endpoint(s) must be detailed in the protocol and SAP; visits and radiological assessments should be symmetric between study arms to prevent systematic bias.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Clinical Trial Design Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: The methodology for assessing, measuring, and analyzing the endpoint(s) should be detailed in the protocol and SAP. Visits and radiological assessments, when applicable, should be symmetric between the two study arms to prevent systematic bias.

rules.rule_text: The FDA and the applicant should agree prospectively on the study design, definition of progression, SAP, methodology for handling missing data and censoring methods, data to be recorded on the CRF, and operating procedures of an IRC, finalizing these items before initiation of the study.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Clinical Trial Design Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: The FDA and the applicant should agree prospectively on the following items and the applicant should finalize the items before the initiation of the study, to the extent possible: The study design; The definition of progression; The SAP; The methodology for handling missing data and censoring methods; The data to be recorded on the case report form (CRF); The operating procedures of an independent endpoint review committee (IRC), if applicable.

rules.rule_text: The analysis plan should specify the primary analysis and one or more sensitivity analyses to evaluate the robustness of the results; missing data and censoring methods should be specified in the protocol.
rules.source_section: Section IV.E — Clinical Trial Analysis Issues
rules.verbatim_quote: The analysis plan should specify the primary analysis and one or more sensitivity analyses to evaluate the robustness of the results. Although any analyses with missing data can be problematic, the results can be strengthened by similar results in both the primary and the sensitivity analyses.
cross_references: ICH E9 Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials

rules.rule_text: Applicants should meet with the FDA before submitting protocols intended to support NDA or BLA marketing applications; applicants may request a special protocol assessment to provide confirmation of the acceptability of endpoints and protocol design.
rules.source_section: Section V — Conclusion
rules.verbatim_quote: we recommend that applicants meet with the FDA before submitting protocols intended to support NDA or BLA marketing applications. The FDA will ensure that these meetings include a multidisciplinary FDA team of oncologists, statisticians, clinical pharmacologists, and external expert consultants as needed. Applicants can submit protocols after these meetings and request a special protocol assessment that provides confirmation of the acceptability of endpoints and protocol design to support drug marketing applications.

rules.rule_text: An independent endpoint review committee (IRC) charter must be in place before initiation of the study; the charter must describe assurance of independence, procedures for collection, storage, and transportation of results, and resolution of interpretation differences.
rules.source_section: Appendix — Independent Review of Tumor Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: A clearly written plan of the charter outlining the IRC function and process (independent review charter) should be in place before initiation of the study. The plan should describe the assurance of the committee's independence and procedure for collection, storage, and transportation of the results. The charter also should include the resolution of differences in interpretation and incorporation of clinical data in the final interpretation of data and audit procedures.
cross_references: FDA guidance Developing Medical Imaging Drug and Biological Products, Part 3: Design, Analysis, and Interpretation of Clinical Studies

rules.rule_text: Surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit; ORR has been the most commonly used surrogate endpoint in support of accelerated approval.
rules.source_section: Section II.B — Endpoints Supporting Past Approvals in Oncology
rules.verbatim_quote: Surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit (FD&C Act § 506(c)(1)(A); 21 CFR part 314, subpart H; and 21 CFR part 601, subpart E). While durable ORR has been used as a traditional approval endpoint in some circumstances, ORR has also been the most commonly used surrogate endpoint in support of accelerated approval.

rules.rule_text: Drug applications using studies that rely on tumor assessment endpoints as sole evidence of efficacy may need confirmatory evidence from a second trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Endpoints Based on Tumor Assessments
rules.verbatim_quote: Drug applications using studies that rely on tumor assessment endpoints as sole evidence of efficacy may need confirmatory evidence from a second trial.

rules.rule_text: Progression-free survival (PFS) assumes that death events are randomly related to tumor progression; TTP censors death events which may introduce informative censoring.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.4 — Time to Progression and Progression-Free Survival
rules.verbatim_quote: PFS assumes that death events are randomly related to tumor progression. In TTP analysis, death events are censored, either at the time of death or at an earlier visit representing informative censoring (nonrandom pattern of loss from the study).

rules.rule_text: Minimal residual disease (MRD) has been used as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and metastasis-free survival has been used as a clinical endpoint for traditional approval; sponsors planning to use emerging endpoints should discuss with FDA prior to initiating a trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.E — Emerging Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: minimal residual disease has been used as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval for a therapy in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and metastasis-free survival has been used as a clinical endpoint for traditional approval for a therapy in non-metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. If a sponsor is planning to use an emerging endpoint in its clinical development program, we recommend discussing such use with the applicable FDA Division or Office prior to initiating a trial.
cross_references: FDA guidance Hematologic Malignancies: Regulatory Considerations for Use in Minimal Residual Disease in Development of Drug and Biological Products for Treatment
